**CampusVote**

Software Design Document

Project Deliverable 3

**Trojan Force**

NeVaeh Dabney-Rich  |  Jordan Daniel  |  Adeiyi Olajide  |  Kyra Evans  |  Munso Bwalya

CSCI 487 – Software Design & Development

Professor: Dr. Muhammad Haris Rais

Due Date: April 12, 2026

# **1\. Introduction**

CampusVote is a web-based application designed to modernize student government elections at Virginia State University (VSU). The platform provides a centralized, secure, and transparent digital system where students can access candidate information, cast votes, and view real-time election results from any internet-connected device.

This Software Design Document (SDD) covers the requirements engineering, system architecture, and design artifacts — including five UML diagrams — that serve as the technical blueprint for the CampusVote implementation phase.

# **2\. System Overview**

CampusVote is a serverless web application consisting of a React-based frontend and a Firebase-based backend. The system utilizes Firebase for real-time data storage, hosting, and authentication. It integrates with VSU Single Sign-On (SSO) to ensure only authorized students can participate in elections.

Two primary user roles exist within the system:

* Student: can log in, view candidate profiles, cast one vote per election, and view results after polls close.

* Admin: can create and manage elections, manage candidate profiles, view a real-time results tracker, and verify student accounts.

# **3\. Requirements Engineering**

## **3.1 Functional Requirements**

The following table documents the functional requirements for CampusVote. Each requirement describes a specific behavior the system must support.

| Req. ID | Description | Priority |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| FR-01 | Students shall authenticate using their VSU SSO credentials before accessing any voting functionality. | High |
| FR-02 | The system shall enforce a one-vote-per-student-per-election rule and reject any duplicate vote submissions. | High |
| FR-03 | Students shall be able to browse all candidate profiles including name, position, biography, and campaign platform. | High |
| FR-04 | Students shall be able to cast a vote for exactly one candidate per position during an open election period. | High |
| FR-05 | The system shall display real-time vote counts and percentages once an election has closed. | High |
| FR-06 | Admins shall be able to create, edit, open, and close elections via a dedicated admin dashboard. | High |
| FR-07 | Admins shall be able to add and manage candidate profiles associated with each election. | Medium |
| FR-08 | Admins shall have access to a live results tracker displaying vote counts during an active election. | Medium |
| FR-09 | The system shall send a confirmation notification to students upon successful vote submission. | Medium |
| FR-10 | Students shall be able to view their own vote history showing candidate selected and election date. | Low |

## **3.2 Non-Functional Requirements**

| Req. ID | Description | Priority |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| NFR-01 | All data transmitted between client and server shall be encrypted using HTTPS/TLS 1.2 or higher. | High |
| NFR-02 | The system shall support a minimum of 500 concurrent users without degradation in response time. | High |
| NFR-03 | The user interface shall be fully responsive across desktop, tablet, and mobile screen sizes. | High |
| NFR-04 | Vote records shall be stored in a tamper-resistant manner; database entries shall not be editable post-submission. | High |
| NFR-05 | Pages shall load within 3 seconds on standard broadband connections. | Medium |
| NFR-06 | Role-based access control shall prevent students from accessing admin-only features or data. | High |
| NFR-07 | All administrative actions shall be logged with timestamps for audit trail purposes. | Medium |

# **4\. System Architecture**

CampusVote follows a modern serverless architecture:

* **Frontend Layer**: React.js SPA with React Router for navigation. The frontend interacts directly with Firebase services using the Firebase SDK.

* **Service Layer (Firebase)**: 
    * **Authentication**: Firebase Authentication manages student logins via VSU SSO integration and handles user sessions.
    * **Cloud Functions**: Server-side logic for sensitive operations like verifying vote eligibility and talling final results.

* **Data Layer (Firestore)**: A NoSQL real-time document database storing election data, candidate profiles, and secure, immutable vote records.

# **5\. UML Diagrams**

Five UML diagrams collectively document the structure, behavior, and infrastructure of CampusVote. Taken together they cover use cases, class relationships, interaction flows, activity logic, and deployment architecture.

## **5.1 Use Case Diagram**

The Use Case Diagram identifies the two primary actors — Student and Admin — and maps out all key system interactions. Students can log in, view candidate profiles, cast a vote, view results, and check their vote status. Admins share the login use case and additionally manage elections, manage candidates, view the results tracker, and verify student accounts.

![Figure 1: Use Case Diagram – CampusVote][image1]

*Figure 1: Use Case Diagram – CampusVote*

## **5.2 Class Diagram**

The Class Diagram defines the core domain model. User is the base class extended by Student and Admin. Vote associates with both Student (a student casts a vote) and Candidate (a candidate receives votes). Election is managed by Admin and serves as the container for all voting activity.

![Figure 2: Class Diagram – CampusVote][image2]

*Figure 2: Class Diagram – CampusVote*

## **5.3 Sequence Diagram**

The Sequence Diagram traces the full message flow for authentication and vote submission. The interaction spans five participants: Student, Browser UI, Web Server, Auth Service, and Database. An alternative flow captures the case where the student has already voted, returning a 409 error response.

![Figure 3: Sequence Diagram – Voting Flow][image3]

*Figure 3: Sequence Diagram – Voting Flow*

## **5.4 Activity Diagram**

The Activity Diagram models the step-by-step process of casting a vote, from opening the application to receiving a confirmation screen. Two decision nodes handle branching: valid credential check (with an error path for invalid login) and duplicate vote check (with an error path for students who have already voted).

![Figure 4: Activity Diagram – Casting a Vote][image4]

*Figure 4: Activity Diagram – Casting a Vote*

## **5.5 Deployment Diagram**

The Deployment Diagram shows how software components are distributed across infrastructure nodes. The Client Device communicates directly with Firebase services over HTTPS. Static assets are delivered via Firebase's global CDN. Authentication is handled by Firebase Auth, and data is persisted in the Firestore Cloud Database. This serverless approach removes the need for managing a physical web or database server.

![Figure 5: Deployment Diagram – CampusVote Infrastructure][image5]

*Figure 5: Deployment Diagram – CampusVote Infrastructure*

# **6\. Design Consistency**

All five UML diagrams are internally consistent and directly traceable to the requirements in Section 3:

* The Use Case Diagram (Figure 1\) establishes the system boundary and actor interactions. Each use case corresponds to one or more functional requirements (FR-01 through FR-10).

* The Class Diagram (Figure 2\) defines the data model that supports those use cases. The Vote class enforces FR-02 (one vote per student) through its Student association. The Election class supports FR-06 and FR-07 through its Admin relationship.

* The Sequence Diagram (Figure 3\) shows how classes interact at runtime. The Auth Service call (steps 3–6) directly supports NFR-01 and NFR-06 by verifying credentials before any vote action is permitted.

* The Activity Diagram (Figure 4\) makes the decision logic of the voting flow explicit and testable, directly mapping to FR-02 and FR-04.

* The Deployment Diagram (Figure 5\) maps non-functional requirements to infrastructure. The isolated Database Server addresses NFR-04 (tamper-resistance). The CDN layer supports NFR-05 (page load performance). JWT middleware on the Web Server enforces NFR-06 (role-based access control).

Implementation in Deliverable 4 will follow this architecture directly, with the class diagram as the basis for the database schema and the sequence/activity diagrams guiding API endpoint logic.

# **7\. Glossary**

| Term | Definition |
| :---- | :---- |
| **SSO** | Single Sign-On – VSU centralized authentication used to verify student identity before granting system access. |
| **Firebase** | A Google-backed platform providing serverless tools like Authentication, Cloud Functions, and NoSQL databases. |
| **Firestore** | A flexible, scalable NoSQL cloud database for mobile, web, and server development from Firebase. |
| **Serverless** | An architecture where the cloud provider manages the server infrastructure, scaling, and maintenance. |
| **HTTPS** | Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure – encrypted communication protocol used for all data in transit. |
| **SPA** | Single Page Application – React architecture where routing occurs client-side without full page reloads. |
